coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots/Ag sensitized TiO2 nanotube film for applications in photocathodic protection
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Zi-Chao
Jin, Piao
Liu, Qing
Wang, Xia
Chen, Li-Fang
Xu, Hui
Song, Guang-Ling
Du, Rong-Gui
coredata.dc:description: In order to obtain a TiO2 composite film with improved photoelectrochemical properties, a TiO2 nanotube array (NTA) film was constructed on a Ti substrate via anodization, then Ag nanoparticles (NPs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successively deposited on the TiO2 NTA film by pulse current deposition and hydrothermal treatment, respectively. After the sensitization by CQDs and Ag NPs, the light absorption of the TiO2 composite film was extended to the visible region. Under white light irradiation, the photocurrent density of the composite film in a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution reached 110 μA cm−2, much higher than that (60 μA cm−2) of the single TiO2 film. The CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film as a photoanode showed an improved photocathodic protection effect, which made the potential of the coupled 403 stainless steel (403SS) in the 0.5 M NaCl solution drop by 400 mV relative to its corrosion potential.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 nanotubes
Pulse electrodeposition
Ag nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dots
Photocathodic protection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the prepared CQDs at (a) low and (b) high magnifications.

Top-view SEM images of the (a) TiO2 NTA film, (b) Ag/TiO2 composite film and (c) CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film, (d) cross-sectional SEM image of CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film. Inset in (a) is the cross-sectional SEM image of the TiO2 NTA film.
TEM images of the (a) TiO2 NTA film, (b) and (c) Ag/TiO2 composite film at (b) low and (c) high magnifications, and (d) the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film.
XRD patterns of the (a) TiO2 NTA film, (b) Ag/TiO2 composite film, and (d) CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film.
EDS spectra of the TiO2 NTA film and CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film.
XPS spectrum of the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) Ti 2p, (b) Ag 3d, (c) C 1s, and (d) O 1s in the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra and (b) PL spectra of the prepared films.
(A) Photocurrent density-wavelength spectra and (B) time evolution of the transient photocurrents of the different films under white light irradiation: (a) TiO2, (b) Ag/TiO2, and (c) CQDs/Ag/TiO2.
Time evolution of the potential of 403SS in a 0.5 M NaCl solution: (a) uncoupled, (b) coupled to the TiO2 film and (c) coupled to the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film under intermittent light irradiation.
Nyquist plots of 403SS in a 0.5 M NaCl solution: (a) uncoupled, (b) coupled to the TiO2 film and (c) coupled to the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film under white light irradiation.
Equivalent circuit model used for EIS data fitting.
Schematic illustration of the separation and transfer of charge carriers in the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film as a photoanode for the photocathodic protection application.
Chemical composition of the TiO2 film and CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots/Ag sensitized TiO2 nanotube film for applications in photocathodic protection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Marine Materials Corrosion and Protection, College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Marine Materials Corrosion and Protection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Marine Materials Corrosion and Protection, College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to obtain a TiO2 composite film with improved photoelectrochemical properties, a TiO2 nanotube array (NTA) film was constructed on a Ti substrate via anodization, then Ag nanoparticles (NPs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successively deposited on the TiO2 NTA film by pulse current deposition and hydrothermal treatment, respectively. After the sensitization by CQDs and Ag NPs, the light absorption of the TiO2 composite film was extended to the visible region. Under white light irradiation, the photocurrent density of the composite film in a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution reached 110 μA cm−2, much higher than that (60 μA cm−2) of the single TiO2 film. The CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film as a photoanode showed an improved photocathodic protection effect, which made the potential of the coupled 403 stainless steel (403SS) in the 0.5 M NaCl solution drop by 400 mV relative to its corrosion potential.

ce:section.ce:para: Since Fujishima and Honda reported that water splitting could be achieved by using TiO2 electrodes under ultraviolet (UV) illumination in 1972 [1], nanostructured TiO2 semiconductor materials have attracted considerable attention for a variety of applications in the field of photoelectrochemistry due to their excellent properties including nontoxicity, chemical inertness, high resistance to photocorrosion, and low cost [2–4]. Among different nanostructured TiO2 materials, the self-organized TiO2 nanotube array (NTA) films fabricated on Ti substrate surfaces by electrochemical anodization show excellent photoelectric conversion performances because of their optimized charge transfer pathways [5]. However, pure TiO2 films can be exploited for photoelectrochemical applications only when they are illuminated by UV light. Moreover, the quick recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the pure TiO2 films is disadvantageous to their applications.

Hence, many methods of preparing TiO2 NTA composite films have been developed to enhance the photoelectrochemical performances of TiO2 films by extending their photoabsorption to the visible light region and suppressing the recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs [6–13]. Noble metal loading, metallic ions and non-metal doping, semiconductor coupling and dye sensitization are often used to modify TiO2 NTA films [6–9]. The noble metal (Ag, Au, Pt, Pd, etc.) loading is an efficient approach to improve the photoelectrochemical performance of TiO2 [9–11]. In a TiO2 NTA film with noble metal loading, a Schottky barriers may be formed at the interface between the metal and TiO2 [12], and effectively suppress the recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Moreover, the noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) loaded on the TiO2 NTA film surface can generate a surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect, and improve the visible light absorption and the photoelectrochemical properties of the TiO2 composite film [13,14]. For example, Zhang et al. [11] reported that the photoinduced current density of the Ag nanoparticle sensitized TiO2 NTA film was almost 20 times higher than that of the single TiO2 film due to the SPR effect of Ag NPs, and the Ag/TiO2 film exhibited excellent photocatalytic properties. Therefore, as one of the most efficient plasmonic metals, Ag is believed to be an attractive candidate for modifying TiO2 NTA films.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs, a new class of carbon nanomaterials) are environmentally friendly, and have up-converted photoluminescence, high efficient photoinduced electron transfer and electron reservoir properties [15–19]. In a CQDs/TiO2 composite semiconductor, CQDs can act as an electron reservoir to reduce the electron-hole pair recombination probability. They also can absorb visible light to emit shorter wavelength light through up-conversion, which in turn further excites TiO2 to produce more electron–hole pairs [15,18,20–22]. That is to say, sensitization of TiO2 NTA films with CQDs can further improve their light absorption ability and facilitate the separation and transfer of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Additionally, CQDs have been used as a reducing or stabilizing agent to synthesize CQDs/Ag composites [23–25]. Thus, CQDs can also improve the stability of Ag/TiO2 NTA films.

According to the above existing studies, CQDs and Ag NPs are likely to have a synergistic effect on optimizing the properties of a CQDs/Ag/TiO2 NTA composite film. However, no investigation on CQDs/Ag/TiO2 films has been reported so far. Therefore, the objective of this work is to fabricate a CQDs/Ag modified TiO2 NTA composite film and investigate its photoelectrochemical properties to validate the synergistic effect of CQDs and Ag NPs. We synthesized a CQDs/Ag/TiO2 NTA composite film by a facile three-step process including anodic oxidation, pulse current deposition and hydrothermal treatment, and found that the composite film showed strong visible light absorption performance and could provide enhanced photocathodic protection for 403 stainless steel (403SS).

A three-step process consisting of anodic oxidation, pulse current deposition, and hydrothermal treatment was used for preparing a CQDs/Ag/TiO2 NTA composite film, which was convenient and easy to operate.

TiO2 NTA films were constructed on Ti substrates by an electrochemical anodic oxidation method. Prior to the anodic oxidation, a Ti foil (0.1 cm thickness, 99.8% purity) was cut into multiple 15 mm × 10 mm pieces and used as specimens, and these pieces of Ti were sequentially sonicated in acetone, ethanol and deionized water for 20 min. A two-electrode system with a Pt sheet auxiliary electrode and the Ti specimen as the working electrode was used in the preparation process. The anodic oxidation was conducted in a deionized water, ethylene glycol, glycerol (1:1:1 in volume) and 0.5 wt% NH4F mixed solution at 20 V for 30 min. The prepared TiO2 NTA film was annealed at 450 °C for 2 h in air.

Ag nanoparticles were deposited onto the TiO2 NTA film through pulse current deposition by an Autolab PGSTAT30 workstation. The deposition was carried out in a two-electrode cell with a mixed solution of 10 mM AgNO3 and 100 mM NaNO3. The prepared TiO2 NTA film and a Pt sheet acted as the working electrode and the auxiliary electrode, respectively. The current density for pulse current deposition was 10 mA cm−2, the pulse duration was 0.1 s and the on/off time ratio was 1:3 (s). The desired Ag/TiO2 NTA film was obtained by 100 deposition cycles. After the deposition, the film was rinsed by deionized water.

CQDs were prepared through a simple hydrothermal process. Specifically, 0.90 g ascorbic acid was dissolved in a 40 mL mixture of deionized water and absolute ethyl alcohol (1:1 in volume). After stirring for 30 min, the mixture solution was transferred to a 50 mL teflon-sealed autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was extracted with 50 mL dichloromethane to get the upper brown aqueous solution. Finally, the extracted solution was adjusted with a 0.1 M NaOH solution to neutral pH, and dialyzed for 24 h to obtain the desired CQDs solution. To deposit CQDs onto the Ag/TiO2 film, the prepared Ag/TiO2 film and the obtained CQDs solution were transferred to a 50 mL teflon-sealed autoclave, and hydrothermally treated at 80 °C for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the sample with CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film was rinsed with deionized water.

The surface morphologies of the prepared films were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai F30). Elemental and crystalline phase analyses were carried out by energy dispersive X-Ray spectroscopy (EDS, attachment to Hitachi S-4800) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ultima IV). The surface chemical state analyses were performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Quantum 2000). The light absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the films were measured by a Cary-5000 UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. The excitation wavelength for the PL measurements was 370 nm.

The photocurrents of the prepared films in a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution were measured in a three-electrode system containing the film as the working electrode, a Pt auxiliary electrode and a saturated calomel reference electrode (SCE). A 150 W Xe lamp was employed as the white light source in the 250–800 nm wavelengths range. The photocurrent spectra of the films were measured by a model 263A potentiostat/galvanostat with an M5210 lock-in amplifier.

The photocathodic protection provided by the prepared films as photoanodes was investigated by using a home-assembled experimental setup including a model 263A potentiostat/galvanostat, a 150 W Xe lamp, a photoelectrochemical cell containing a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution and a corrosion cell containing a 0.5 M NaCl solution. The photoanode was placed in the photoelectrochemical cell, and a 403 stainless steel (403SS) working electrode (exposed area of 1 cm2), a Pt auxiliary electrode and an SCE were stalled in the corrosion cell. The photoanode was electrically connected to the 403SS electrode by a copper wire, and the photoelectrochemical and corrosion cells were connected via a salt bridge. The potentials of the 403SS coupled with the film photoanode were recorded under intermittent illumination conditions to evaluate the photocathodic protection performance of the film.

Fig. 1 presents the typical TEM images of the prepared CQDs. Some small particles with size of about 5 nm could be observed from TEM image shown in Fig. 1a. These small particles could be recognized as quantum dots (QDs) based on their sizes [26]. From the high-resolution TEM image shown in Fig. 1b, we could observe that these particles had a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which corresponded to the (100) facet of graphite [27].

Fig. 2 a shows the SEM image of the anodized TiO2 NTA film. The film contained ordered single-wall TiO2 nanotubes with about 100 nm internal diameters and 12–15 nm wall thicknesses. After the deposition of Ag NPs, uniformly distributed particles could be observed on the NTA film surface (Fig. 2b), and therefore the Ag/TiO2 composite film showed a rough film surface. Moreover, after the hydrothermal deposition of CQDs, many other small particles were found on the film surface (Fig. 2c). Fig. 2d shows the cross-sectional SEM image of the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film, where the particles could be observed on the TiO2 nanotube surfaces. Fig. 3 presents TEM images of the TiO2, Ag/TiO2 and CQDs/Ag/TiO2 films. In Fig. 3a, a complete morphology of the pure TiO2 nanotubes could be observed, indicating that the TiO2 film was composed of nanotubes. After the deposition of Ag, obvious nanoparticles could be seen on the TiO2 nanotube in Fig. 3b, and the corresponding high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 3c) shows a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm which could be ascribed to the (111) facet of cubic Ag [28]. From the TEM image of the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film (Fig. 3d), obvious Ag and C nanoparticles could be observed. The sizes of the Ag NPs and CQDs were 15–20 nm and 6–7 nm, respectively, implying that the Ag NPs or CQDs were not agglomerated during the deposition processes.

Fig. 4 displays the XRD patterns of the TiO2, Ag/TiO2 and CQDs/Ag/TiO2 films. For all the films, the main diffraction peaks at 35.2°, 38.5°, 40.3°, 53.1°, 63.0°, 70.7° and 76.3° could be ascribed to Ti (PDF #89–2762), while diffraction peaks at 25.4° and 48.1° could be indexed to the (101) and (200) facets of anatase TiO2 (PDF #89–4921), respectively. As shown in Fig. 4b and c, the two diffraction peaks at 38.2° and 44.4° corresponded to the (111) and (200) lattice planes of Ag, respectively (PDF #87–0718). The (111) lattice plane found from the XRD measurement was consistent with the high-resolution TEM observation (Fig. 3c), indicating the deposition of Ag NPs on the TiO2 film. However, no diffraction peak belonging to CQDs was detected from the XRD pattern, which was mainly due to the low content, high dispersion and low crystallinity of CQDs in the composite film [29,30].

From the EDS spectra of TiO2 and CQDs/Ag/TiO2 films shown in Fig. 5 , elements Ti, O and C were detected, and Ag could be found only in the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 film. The C in the TiO2 film was attributed to contamination from the experimental environments. The contents of the different elements of the films are listed in Table 1 . It can be seen that the weight and atomic percentages of Ag in the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film were 3.11% and 0.87%, respectively. After the deposition of CQDs, the weight and atomic percentages of C in the composite film were higher than those in the TiO2 film, respectively, indicating the existence of CQDs in the composite film.

XPS measurements were conducted to determine the compositions and their valence states for the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 NTA composite film. From the overall XPS spectrum of the composite film shown in Fig. 6 , Ti 2p, Ti 3p, O 1s, O 2s, Ag 3d and C 1s peaks could be detected, indicating that the composite film was mainly composed of Ti, O, Ag and C elements. High-resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p, Ag 3d, C 1s and O 1s are presented in Fig. 7 . The strong peaks of Ti 2p with binding energies of 457.7 eV and 463.4 eV were identiﬁed as Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 (Fig. 7a), respectively, and attributed to Ti4+ in the composite film [31,32]. As shown in the XPS spectrum of Ag 3d (Fig. 7b), the two characteristic Ag 3d peaks located at 368.3 eV and 374.3 eV were attributed to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively, which conﬁrmed the existence of metallic Ag (Ag0) in the composite film [13,33,34]. The C 1s peak in Fig. 7c could be deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 285.0 eV, 285.9 eV and 287.9 eV, respectively. The peak with binding energy of 285.0 eV was attributed to the C–C bond with a sp2 orbital [15,35], which was related to the film surface contamination introduced by air exposure before the XPS measurement [36]. It is noteworthy that the peaks at 285.9 eV and 287.9 eV were assigned to the C–O and CO bonds, respectively, originating from the CQDs [15,17,22]. Moreover, no peak of a Ti–C bond was detected in Fig. 7a and c, indicating that the CQDs did not act as a dopant in the composite film. In Fig. 7d, the broad and strong peak could be resolved into the three peaks, in which the characteristic Ti–O peak appeared at 528.9 eV, while the other two shoulder peaks located at 529.6 eV and 530.9 eV were assigned to C–O and CO bonds from CQDs [15,23,37], respectively. The results shown in Fig. 7c and d indicated the existence of carbon-oxygen bonds in the composite film.

Based on the above morphological observations and chemical composition analyses, it can be concluded that the desired CQDs/Ag/TiO2 NTA composite film was successfully prepared by the above three-step synthesis method.

Light absorption ability of the prepared films was evaluated via the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. As presented in Fig. 8 a, all the films showed strong absorption in the UV region, which was related to the intrinsic band gap absorption of the anatase TiO2. However, their light absorption performances differed from each other in the visible light region. The single TiO2 NTA film had almost no light absorption to visible light. The small absorption peaks of the single TiO2 film were possibly attributed to the trapped holes and electrons at the sub-band gap state of the TiO2 NTA film [38]. However, the Ag/TiO2 and CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite films showed quite strong light absorption in the visible region, and the corresponding absorption band between 400 and 700 nm was formed for the two composite films. Some obvious absorption peaks appeared at around 400–600 nm, which were ascribed to the SPR eﬀect of spatially confined electrons in the Ag NPs [13,39,40]. In addition, the light absorption intensity of the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 film showed a further increase and the absorption peaks were slightly red-shifted compared to that of the Ag/TiO2 film. This was probably because that the CQDs could absorb visible light and emit shorter wavelength light to further excite TiO2 [16,20–22]. Therefore, the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film exhibited the best light absorption performance, which was of significance for its photoelectrochemical applications.

PL spectra are often utilized to study the transfer behavior of the photogenerated carriers in solid semiconductor materials because they can provide information of charge separation and recombination. Fig. 8b shows the PL spectra of the prepared films at excitation wavelength 370 nm. It can be clearly seen that the single TiO2 NTA film showed the highest PL emission intensity, indicating that charge carriers in this film were more inclined to recombine. However, the PL emission intensities decreased significantly for the Ag/TiO2 and CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite films. This phenomenon could be explained as follows: Ag had a higher work function (4.7 eV) than TiO2 (4.0 eV) [41], thus the photoelectrons in the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 would transfer to Ag NPs to reach the Fermi level equilibrium, which resulted in the formation of a Schottky barrier at their interface. As a result, the charge carrier recombination was effectively suppressed, and the PL emission intensity decreased [11]. Especially, compared to the Ag/TiO2 film, the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 film showed lower PL emission intensities. The presence of CQDs could promote the transfer of the photogenerated electrons due to its excellent conductivity, and facilitate the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, resulting in the low PL emission intensities of the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 film.

Fig. 9 A presents the photocurrent density vs. wavelength spectra for the prepared films in the 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution. The three different films all showed their maximum photocurrent densities at around 330 nm, which was blue-shifted relative to the gap of bulk TiO2 (387 nm), probably owing to the quantum confinement effect [40,42]. The CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film showed the maximum photocurrent density of 123.4 μA cm−2, which was higher than the Ag/TiO2 (109.5 μA cm−2) or single TiO2 (93.0 μA cm−2) film. In addition, there was no obvious photoelectrochemical response for the single TiO2 film in the visible region, while both Ag/TiO2 and CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite films showed the photocurrent responses in the range of 400–550 nm and the peak values at around 475 nm. Fig. 9B shows the time evolution of the transient photocurrent densities of the prepared films under white light irradiation. The photocurrent densities of the films immediately changed after the light was on or off, showing their high sensitivity to white light. After several on/off cycles of irradiation, the photocurrent response of the three films was reproducible, indicating that the films had good stability. Similar to the results from the photocurrent density vs. wavelength spectra, the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film showed the highest photocurrent density of about 110 μA cm−2 among the three films (40 μA cm−2 for the TiO2 film, and 60 μA cm−2 for the Ag/TiO2 film). Compared with the single TiO2 film, the enhanced photocurrent response of the Ag/TiO2 composite film might be attributed to the SPR effect of the metallic Ag in the composite film. The metallic Ag could be excited by visible light, and the resulted photoelectrons were transferred to the TiO2 through the Schottky junction formed at the Ag/TiO2 interface [11,33,43–46]. For the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film, besides the SPR effect of Ag nanoparticles, CQDs could absorb visible light to produce photoelectrons, and emit shorter wavelength light due to the up-conversion property [15,20,29,47]. The shorter wavelength luminescence could further excite TiO2 to produce more photoelectrons. The higher photocurrent response implied the lower charge carrier recombination rate and the higher photoelectric conversion efﬁciency [34]. The photocurrent density of the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film was higher than that of the Ag/TiO2 composite film, suggesting that the introduction of CQDs could effectively promote the transfer of the photogenerated electrons. These results were consistent with the PL measurements.

Photocathodic protection by semiconductor materials is equivalent to a cathodic protection effect for corrosion control of metals [7,32,48,49], which does not need supplied currents or sacrificial anodes, and has drawn much attention in the in the field of corrosion and protection of metals.

Based on the above analyses, the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film showing the best photoelectrochemical performance was selected as a photoanode to investigate its photocathodic protection effect on 403SS. In a photocathodic protection system, usually, the potential change of the metal to be protected is recorded to evaluate the photocathodic protection effect of the photoanode coupled with the metal [32,48,49]. Fig. 10 presents the time evolution of the 403SS potential under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 10a, before photocathodic protection tests, the corrosion potential (open circuit potential) of the uncoupled 403SS in the 0.5 M NaCl solution changed in a small range from 70 to 110 mV (vs. SCE). The potential of the 403SS shifted negatively to about −10 mV (vs. SCE) and −190 mV (vs. SCE) when it was coupled to the single TiO2 film and the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film, respectively. The decreases in the potentials were caused by the galvanic effects. These results suggested that the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film had a more negative open circuit potential than the single TiO2 NTA film. When the film photoanode was irradiated by white light, the potential of the coupled 403SS was further shifted negatively. For the 403SS coupled with the single TiO2 film, its potential decreased to about −250 mV (vs. SCE). Especially, the potential of the 403SS coupled with the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film decreased to −330 mV (vs. SCE), and was about 400 mV lower than its corrosion potential. The decreases in the potential indicated that the 403SS was under cathodic protection (namely photocathodic protection in this study). This was because many generated electrons of the film photoanode under white light irradiation were transferred to the 403SS and made it cathodic polarization. The CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film showed more effective photocathodic protection than the single TiO2 film, suggesting that the sensitization with Ag NPs and CQDs could improve the photocathodic protection performance of the TiO2 composite film. Once the light was cut off, the 403SS potential returned to the level before light irradiation, meaning that the photogenerated electron supply from the film was stopped under the dark condition, and the potential of the coupled 403SS correspondingly increased. These phenomena imply that the prepared films are very sensitive to the light.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is commonly used to study the interfacial electrochemical behavior between the metal and electrolyte, and investigate the corrosion performance of the metal [32,50]. The EIS measurements of the 403SS electrode uncoupled or coupled with the photoanodes were carried out in the 0.5 M NaCl solution without applied potentials. The Nyquist plots of the 403SS are presented in Fig. 11 . Only one capacitive arc was visible on each Nyquist plot, indicating that the EIS data could be fitted by using a simple three-element equivalent circuit described in Fig. 12 . In the circuit model, R ct and R s represent the charge transfer resistance and the solution resistance, respectively. A constant phase element (CPE) is used to characterize the capacitance at the steel/solution interface for the non-ideal capacitive behavior [32,51]. The R ct value may be employed to examine the electrochemical reaction resistance between the steel and the solution. After fitting the EIS results, the R ct of the uncoupled 403SS was as high as 438.2 kΩ cm2, but it decreased to 58.30 kΩ cm2 and 12.4 kΩ cm2 when the 403SS was coupled to the single TiO2 film and the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film under white light irradiation, respectively. The obvious decrease in R ct for the 403SS coupled with the each film photoanode demonstrated that the electrochemical reaction at the steel/solution interface was accelerated, which was mainly attributed to the photogenerated electron transfer from the film to the 403SS. When the film was irradiated by white light, electrons in the film were excited from its valence band (VB) to its CB, and were transferred to the 403SS surface to take part in the electrochemical reactions (mainly oxygen reduction) [48], and therefore the R ct value decreased significantly. The participation of photogenerated electrons in the reaction greatly accelerated the cathodic reaction, and retarded the anodic reaction (namely anodic dissolution) on the 403SS surface. This means that the 403SS corrosion was reduced because of the photocathodic protection by the TiO2 or CQDs/Ag/TiO2 film. The lower R ct value implied the higher photocathodic protection efficiency. Apparently, the R ct of the 403SS coupled with the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film was lower than that coupled with the TiO2 film, and therefore the composite film had a more effective photocathodic protection effect on 403SS.

Based on the above experimental results and discussion, a possible charge sepration and transfer mechanism of the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 NTA composite film for photocathodic protection is illustrated in Fig. 13 . There were three charge sepration and transfer paths in the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film under white light irradiation. First, due to the SPR eﬀect, Ag NPs were excited by visible light to produce electron-hole pairs [11,33,43–46]. The excited electons were transferred to the CB of TiO2, while photogenerated holes were consumed by hole scavengers in the solution. Second, CQDs could also be excited by visible light to produce electron-hole pairs, and the excited electons were similarly injected into the TiO2 CB. Moreover, CQDs could absorb visible light and near-infrared light (>600 nm), and then emit shorter wavelength light (<380 nm) through up-convertion property [15,20,29,47]. The emitted shorter wavelength light (luminescence) could further excite TiO2 to produce electron-hole pairs. Third, the excited electrons could transfer between the Ag NPs and the CQDs to inhibit the recombination of the photogenerated electrons and holes [29,33], and finally were injected into the TiO2 CB. Meanwhile, TiO2 itself could absorb UV light to excite its VB electrons to the CB. Afterwards, the photogenerated electons accumulated in the TiO2 CB were further transferred to the 403SS electrode through an external circuit and made its potential decrease resulting from cathodic polarization, while the photogenerated holes were consumed by hole scavengers in the solution. Therefore, the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 NTA composite film had excellent photoelectrochemical properties and could provide more effective photocathodic protecton to 403SS compared to the TiO2 or Ag/TiO2 NTA film.

In summary, a CQDs and Ag NPs co-sensitized TiO2 NTA composite film has been fabricated by the facile three-step process consisting of anodic oxidation, pulse current deposition, and hydrothermal treatment. After the deposition of the CQDs and Ag NPs on the anatase TiO2 NTA film, the composite film remained an ordered NTA structure, and contained 3.11 wt% Ag. Compared to the TiO2 film, the C content increased in the CQD/Ag/TiO2 composite film, indicating the presence of the CQDs.

Because of the SPR effect of Ag NPs and the up-conversion property of CQDs, the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film showed the greatly enhanced light absorption and photoelectrochemical performances. The photoabsorption of the composite film was extended to the visible light region compared with that of the single TiO2 NTA film. Under white light irradiation, the photocurrent density of the composite film in a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution reached 110 μA cm−2, which was about 1.8 times as high as that of the single TiO2 film. In particular, the potential of 403SS in a 0.5 M NaCl solution decreased by 400 mV and 320 mV relative to its corrosion potential when it was coupled to the CQDs/Ag/TiO2 composite film and single TiO2 film, respectively, indicating that the CQDs/Ag sensitized TiO2 composite film could offer more effective photocathodic protection for the steel.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21573182, 51731008, 21173177, 21621091, and J1310024).
